md: **Slope** is just a fancy word for *how slanted a line is*. A nearly-flat line has a small slope; a nearly-vertical line has a huge one. Slope captures both **how steep** the line is and **which way it tilts**.
To measure slope, walk along the line one step at a time. Each step has two parts:
the **run** (how far you move horizontally) and the **rise** (how far you move vertically).
Drag the two points below to make a line. The dotted triangle shows the rise and run between them. Try to predict the slope before reading the answer.
If a line passes through points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$, the slope formula gives the answer directly:
It doesn't matter which point you call $1$ and which you call $2$ — as long as you're consistent in numerator and denominator.

blocks.title: Worked example
blocks.body: A line passes through $(1, 2)$ and $(4, 11)$. Then $m = \dfrac{11 - 2}{4 - 1} = \dfrac{9}{3} = 3$.

md: ### What does the sign tell you?
**Positive slope** ($m > 0$) — the line goes *up* as you read left to right.
**Negative slope** ($m < 0$) — the line goes *down* as you read left to right.
**Zero slope** ($m = 0$) — the line is *horizontal*, no rise.
**Undefined slope** — the line is *vertical*, no run, you'd be dividing by zero.

blocks.title: The bigger the |m|, the steeper the line
blocks.body: $m = 5$ is steeper than $m = 2$. $m = -10$ is steeper than $m = 1$. Sign tells you direction; magnitude tells you steepness.

md: Slope is what makes a line a *line*. Once you know one point on a line and its slope, the entire line is determined. That's the seed idea behind every form of a linear equation — slope-intercept, point-slope, and standard form all encode the same two pieces of information in different ways.
